sentences.text: Yes.
There were different kind of barracks in different camps, all in Auschwitz, but still different.
Some of them were, were from stones-- or not stones, but bricks.
Some of them-- most of them was from wood.
Where I did go, that was plain, simple wood, the roof not even completely finished.
The windows were cut out.
But there were no glass in it and nothing on the floor.
So we did lay on the floor.
But I mean, that was no bridge or bed or blanket or pillow or anything.
And the, the 3B where I was, we had not even a toilet, but a big hole in end of the two line of barracks, because that was two line facing each other, barracks.
I don't know, I think about eight or ten barracks in one line and same number in the-- in one square, like let's say that room.
And by that wall and that wall were the lined up the barracks.
The very end, there was a big hole.
And the-- over the hole were a piece of plywood or, or maybe hard wood, I don't know, thrown over.
And that was our bathroom.
And SS men with a German Shepherd sat in one corner watching you.
So if anyone had to go out, you walked on that narrow wood panel.
And many, many people fell into it.
And nobody ever even tried to help somebody.
You couldn't.
We couldn't help.
The SS men were standing there-- you would not believe it-- with the dog, like nothing happened.
And people were choking and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The whole camp-- I don't know what was your question-- but the whole camp that I was in Auschwitz was mud.
When that was raining, you did go into almost knee-high in the mud.
When that was dry, that was hard like stone.
I never saw there a grass or tree or anything.

So I don't know why they had, had to have so many patrolling SS men with dogs and watchmen-- two watchmen in the watch door watching every movement, what was going on, when we were surrounded by electrified wire, SS men with guns, and a German shepherd ready to jump on you.
We had nowhere to go.
We had no clothes.
We had no hair.
What were you wearing?
Once a month in Auschwitz just, they took us to back to the crematorium to take a shower.
And each time, you get some rags.
So each time, you had something different clothes on you.
First time, I got a man's shirt.
And I was naked.
Next time, I got something else.
I don't even remember what.
I know one, they had a big typhus epidemic.
Or we were inflicted with typhus.
And they were asking people to come and take their dirty clothes back to the de-- disinfection place.
I did volunteer or was taken.
I don't remember.
I just remember I was taken to the place to drop off the clothes.
And I saw open the [? gliding ?] room, you know, where the clothes were when you arrived and everything was taken off from you.
They had a place to put that.
I did go in.
I put about five or six clothes on myself in seconds.
And when I came back to, to the camp, I gave it to my friends and even one lady who, whom I didn't know.
She was almost completely naked.
And that was a long skirt, buttons the front.
I remember that because she said I saved her life with that.
Because she could wear it like a cape.
And she could wear it like a dress or a blanket or whatever.
That's why I remember what kind of a clothes that was.
I don't remember what junk we got, so wearing whatever we got.
Many times-- that was later when we did go to clean the streets in, in German cities, when you found in bombed out places some clothes, what we could put it on ourselves.
But the SS woman realized that is some clothes on you.
They took it away from you.
Yes.
Managed?
I don't know.
I don't know.
You see, everything I remember so clearly.
And there are things just I can't remember at all.
I don't remember when I ever did go to the bathroom.
And I must have.
I just don't remember how I manged it.
I remember very well when other people fell into.
I saw that.
I remember one woman who did go because had typhus.
And we were standing roll call hours and hours, ask why we couldn't go anywhere.
But the German-- the SS men counted us.
And one was missing.
And the so-called blockalteste, you know, the woman, also an inmate from the camp, were responsible for us to be there.
She was-- she was the one who had to count us first.
And she was the one who had to find people.
So we were waiting for one person.
And she was running all over.
Finally, she found the woman next to the toilet, doubled up-- she couldn't even walk anymore-- and dragged her to the role call.
And the SS men said that she was trying to escape.
So he shot her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So things like that, I remember.
Because that some very special reason.
But others, I don't know how I don't remember at all, ever going there in that particular place.
What about food?
What did you get to eat?
Through the whole year, the menu was the same.
Every morning, we had to stand up or roll calls, five in one line, like soldiers, and waiting for the SS men, two, three hours at least, till they came to our camp.
And then after they left, they brought in inmates two can of like the milk can in a farm, containers with black water.
We called it coffee.
The first girls of the five had one empty can that is-- that was [? gas, ?] you know, before it and that empty.
We got it as a dish.
And they ladled into black water.
And the first girl took five sip, and the next one, and the next one, till the five got her coffee, if she was lucky, the last one, to left anything.
That was our breakfast, lunch, and din-- and, and all the water we got a day in that particular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camp.
Evening, the same story repeated.
We stood in a roll call.
And after the roll call, we received a piece of bread exactly the size of my inner palm and so thick like my thumb and like clay.
In the first week, I made statute out of it.
I don't know from what that bread was made.
And we got so-called soup, similar to the breakfast coffee, you know?
I never found anything.
Some people did find cabbage in it.
I did find tree leaves, tree branches, small stones and things like that.
And that was our food.

After the war, we, we learned that Switzer-- Switzerland for Christmas sent to the inmates, I think all of at the front line or wherever, some marmalade-- you know what marmalade i-- and a little butter and extra bread for Christmas.
We never received it.
So that was our food for a whole year, as long as I was in camp.
Later, I was transported out from Auschwitz.
I didn't know then that because the Russians were, were almost there.
That was end of October.
And one crema-- crematorium was blown up by the inmates.
And the, the SS men wanted every Jews out of Auschwitz so that shouldn't happen that I should be liberated by the Russian.
So that's why they rushed us out and taken us to Bergen-Belsen.
This was 1944, end of October, yes.
In Auschwitz, that was a non-existing work.
Men group came over to the camp where I was, because we were naturally all women, and cut out squares of the earth, like here in America you can buy grass rolled up, the squares.
That was exactly the same way, just you couldn't roll because that was very hard earth.
And that's what we had to carry, put on our two hands and carry to the place where the SS men lived.
Because they loved flowers and wanted to have a flowerbed around their barracks.
It was very far from our barracks.
And we-- I never could straighten up because that was too heavy for me.
So I was completely bent and carried this stuff.
If somebody dropped it, depend on the SS man, shot or beat the person.
That was our work.
And took us Bergen-Belsen.
When we arrived, that was horrible rain-- in Auschwitz also.
That was raining for weeks.
And you did go into the mud whenever you did go out.
And that was horrible cold.
And we had no clothes, just one single whatever was on us.
No, we didn't.
I had once a man's shoes.
And each time I was walking, you know, the mud came into the shoes.
And my feet was always ice cold and frozen.
Then later, I got a wooden shoes that were no different.
And that was the shoes.
And we put it under our head so nobody should steal that valuable things what you had.
And in, in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I just try to think about Auschwitz that, OK, that's all right.
In Auschwitz, you know, as the autumn started to come and the cold and the rain, that was earlier dark.
This was a horrible things.
I never saw the sky.
That was always covered with smoke.
And you couldn't avoid not to see the chimney with the flame, red flame, pulling out with the smoke together.
That's what you saw.
And I closed my eye, and I was at home in my thought, in my own city, with, with my parents and my sister and brothers together.
I felt the warm of the house.
I heard the music.
So I, I was normal.
I knew I was in the camp.
I knew that I was in, in, in concentration camp.
But somehow, I didn't feel ever that I was in jail or in a-- an inmate.
I always lived in two places, you know, in my imagination.
That's the way I survived, I think, Auschwitz.
And I was going from barracks to barracks, always telling the girls not to give up, because we will be free.
And evil cannot win.
And sometimes, they almost choked me, because my prediction, that how can I say things like that, and got angry at me.
But somehow, I never gave up hope and faith.
And from there, I did go to, to Bergen-Belsen.
We stayed in tents, because in the buildings were not enough space.
The tent was just tied down.
But between the tent and the ground were a big gap.
So the water from all the places were running in.
So inside, the mud was exactly as big as outside.
We couldn't lay down, naturally.
We were sitting and supporting each other.
By the way, that was in Auschwitz, too, in the B3, supporting each other with each other's back.
You know, we were sitting back to each other.
And that's the way we kept each other up.
And from Bergen-Belsen later, suddenly a lot of people disappeared.
We didn't know they were all cremated or, or shot, whatever.
And then finally, we came into the barracks.
There, we didn't spend too much time, because we were taken to other places to work.
And that's the way I came to Braunschweig.
That was a beautiful city.
I remember early morning, we were going in the dark to work.
And you could see the homes, houses, because it's just a silhouette.
And one wall clearly before my eyes, because that was some big building full of statues on the top.
And one wall with the statues was still standing.
I don't know how come that didn't fell off of something.
And in our-- in Braunschweig, I had the most happy moment in my life when I was cleaning the street with the group, naturally.
And a few street-- I have to tell you, a few street farther, there were Russian soldiers, inmates, who were cleaning also the debris after the bombing.
But they had they warm uniform.
And they had fire, allowed to have a campfire.
We never had any place to warm ourselves.
And the happy moment was because I was very sick.
And I found a place as I was cleaning, what was a, a shelter, but completely bombed house.
And the shelter was under the house.
And I walked on that.
There was a couch.
I laid on there.
And I fell asleep.
Then I had typhus.
I was very sick.
And I didn't even hear the sirens or anything.
And I heard my mother calling, telling me that, that I should go because my father want to talk to me.
And I didn't wanted to get up from the couch.
I was so warm and cozy.
But my mother was calling me again.
I did get up and walked out from the, the shelter.
And then I saw no one single soul in the street.
I knew the church where they always took us.
That was also bombed.
But there still was the building where they took us.
And all the SS men always disappeared.
And as I came to the street level up, that was a beautiful day.
I never forget that silver airplane flying over the sky and dropping bombs to the city.
And I was so happy.
I started to dance and run toward the church, because I knew the rest of the group is sad.
When I arrived, my girlfriend, who is still alive and she's living in New York, screamed at me, we were so scared for you.
Because we didn't know where you are and know how did you dare to stay behind and so on.
So that's what was our work, to clean the streets after the bombing.
No, that was. that was after Bergen-Belsen.
Because we were just three weeks in Bergen-Belsen.
And--
No, no.
In Bergen-Belsen, we were just dying.
I remember one woman who was, every morning Bergen-Belsen, stood up with a little prayer book.
That's what she rescued, so tiny.
And she knew the prayer by heart.
But every morning she stood up outside under the sky and prayed from beginning to the end.
And one day, she was standing there with the prayer book in the hand.
And she was dead.
She froze in the mud, you know, where it did go out.
That was-- we did go deep into it, but we couldn't pull up our feet, most of the.
You know, because we were too weak already.
So she died standing up, praying.
I always wanted to paint that.
But I didn't dare.
Bec-- I painted these pictures, what is here.
And because I painted one of the camp, what I saw.
And I didn't dare to stay in the same room with the picture.
So finally, my husband destroyed the painting.
And there are quite a few pictures in my mind, what I wish to paint.
But somehow I don't, I don't dare to paint.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
I think about two months-- time is something what I don't know, because we never had a calender.
We never knew when is Friday or, or, or not Friday or which month or what.
But I think we were about two months.
But first, we were in Bergen-Belsen, I think about on November.
And end of November or so, we did go to Braunschweig.
And then we there till, I think, middle of February, so about two, two and a half months, roughly.
And from Braunschweig, we did go to another place where that was surprisingly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big, terrible, big building.
I don't know what was that originally, a school or whatever.
And the big [? saw ?] where they had the roll call inside.
That was the only place that the roll call was inside.
And we did work in a factory, what was 500 feet or meter down on earth.
But from the building, we had to go quite a distance.
And from there, we had to go with an elevator down.
And from there, we did go 110 steps down in the salt mine and 110 steps coming up.
And next to that building, I was talking about where we stayed.
That was a private house, also used by us-- I mean, by the SS.
And we called that the hospital.
And one of my friend, because ten girls-- two, five, you know, who were standing in, in roll call every morning and every evening by each other, one of the girl's feet and hands froze.
At-- in Braunschweig, that was open.
And she hardly could stand up.
In Braunschweig, she wanted to stay inside in the barracks to go back to Braunschweig.
And I didn't allowed her.
Because I said, if you stay in, when I come back from work, you will be nowhere.
So I wrapped her arm around my arm.
And I was pulling her to work every single morning.
And that was very painful, especially for her in that cold weather.
But we made it.
But when we came to the other place, she couldn't stand up anymore.
So she was put into the hospital.
One day, the kapo told us that the hospital will be taken away.
And we knew that means they will be taken to the crematorium or, or they will be shot.
So that night, the very same night when I heard that news, I walked through that open-- not open.
The building was closed.
But that was a big, open room.
Nobody was there.
That usually was roll called.
You know, roll call was there.
I walked through that room.
And an SS man was standing outside of the door.
But that was a [INAUDIBLE] alarm, an air raid-- air raid.
So everything had to be pitch dark.
And I opened the door and walked by the SS man.
He didn't notice me in the dark.
And I gone to the hospital to see my friend.
When I did go in, she even talks about today that I was the only one who dared to go over.
And I said goodbye to her.
I said, you, you will be OK.
I didn't know if she knows that they were planned to be killed.
So I did tell her we-- she will be OK.
And I said goodbye to her.
I came back.
And as I came in the door and walked middle of that long, empty room, a light came into my face.
And that was an SS man and a block [? cover, ?] the head of the block, head of the barracks, that Polish-Jewish woman who were responsible for us.
She got very nervous, because you couldn't leave even the room where you were, not, not the building.
And the SS man asked me, where were you?
Where, where did you go?
And I did tell.
I somehow didn't feel any-- anything, fear or anything.
I told him that I heard that the hospital will be taken away.
And I wanted to say goodbye to my friend.
And he didn't say a word.
The block [? cover ?] told me, the Jewish lady, that you will get it, you know, that I will be punished, and get back to your place.
So I did go back.
And next morning, I didn't get coffee.
And next evening, I didn't get any bread and so-called soup.
And she gave me a slap on my face in the morning that I just lay on the floor.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's about it.
I mean, the SS man didn't punish me.
